poem:
Rain on old city windows
I left my home in the early morning, before dawn,
before I was supposed to leave. 
The streetlights were still illuminated and a light frost
gave the world a delicate shimmer.
My shoes made taptap-tapatap as I hurried down the sidewalk.

***

Great woodsman, with churlish face,
and hands that smell of sweat and soil—
where do you find us? In what forest
dark and nameless?

*** 

Returning to the soliloquy of a wandering mind
that wanders too far and gets lost among
the stars or forgotten diction in an Italian
vernacular.

***

Dark liquid within a transparent being

***

A ferocious beast claws at my leg but I keep running. The trees are too close together here, and the branches touch overhead forming a tunnel of leaves. My breath is loud in my ears. Great lung, fill yourself with air! And now, little heart, beat!

***

Dogged simpleton, with loose skin around your neck—
from where did you come? From under what chair? 

***

Light sleeper, wake me when the sun begins to rise. (And bring a cup of freshly brewed coffee.) 

***
	
		I say,

Rain on old city windows!

prompt: A lighthouse speaking to the sea
poem:
A lighthouse speaking to the sea
the only witness a passing ship

I climbed to the top and waited 
for your voice, my chanted Hebrew 

we first spoke in 2013,
beside the Mediterranean


Michael Bottari: guitar



***

(whispered) Kacha Hutchison: violin




***


Kacha Hutchinson: viola





***
(screamed) Michael Bottari: guitar






***

(wailed) Kacha Hutchison: cello







***
(murmured) All: harmonized 






***

(stifled sobbing)












2 table-spoons of honey
in a cup of tea
a recipe for happiness 


3 glasses at most
of wine or arak
before things get interesting 




he returns to Israel every few years
to see his family, and to touch the earth 
that gave birth to him—gave life to 
his crazy messianic imaginings—that 
urgency in the desert, among friends, 
sitting on pillows, smoking in the night. 
He speaks quickly, laughing with wild eyes.
We are preparing for the coming 
messiah, he says, not with words but deeds! 
With open arms, he dances around the room.

She sits by the window, looking out.

Morning after a long winter
I dreamed I had climbed 
to the top of my imagination 
where I could see, in daylight,
the blood coming through. 

Like Paul Revere’s house standing 
beside the North End bricks and traffic,
my heart beats American.

***

The median center of our city
a dark fountain sprouting yellow flowers

and from within the petals a chorus of wings


***



Her February face, solemn and magenta,
standing by the window as the last snow
fell silent and easy as an arrangement
of pencils on a desk.


***
	
	I wash my knives in the sink		and think			of all the things 	that could be done	with a pocketknife at least	a few inches long.
	

***

Returning to the forest where, twenty years ago,
I first kissed her, slipping off to Illinois
again, with my shoes loose and too big,

and again, and then again, and once more for good measure.



***

Darkness inside the shadow of a being
that, looking outwards, sees only light. 


***

Great Lark sparrow, you sleep among us
with your pink feet and feathered egg
do you know our dreams are shared? 



***

Though I have seen wild turkeys wadd

today is the day
I will write a sonnet.

hunting for the wriggling beast 
inside my skin, I look to where it bled,
and seeing only a scabbing over
that didn't need to have been there if not for
the silly thing. It's an obsession really,
like how certain orchestra strings are missing and
you can tell from the sound of the A major chord.
The delusions kick in around two in the morning,
when you think someone is at the door or a roommate has turned on the sink faucet very slightly so as to wake you up but with a more acceptable excuse than just plain old "snores." The delusions want to take over completely, they build skyscrapers out of pizza and make you spit when yawning. You don’t know what else to do with all this energy that wants to be let out somehow—some wild thing—that needs to be tied off like a dangling sentence that trails into the next one without a period or comma or even a dash—to keep going and flowing until the mess is revealed and everyone’s embarrassed because nobody had the foresight to bring snacks at first and now we’re stuck here in this car with no destination and no map and almost out of gas

prompt: I went to the river and wept
poem:
I went to the river and wept
and you didn't even know me

a secret self, remaining 
consistent through changes of letter 

just imagine if a friend came by,
murmuring things not heard since 1999


the great tree is withered now
but I continue searching for its home

inside an envelope, a poem
for your wedding day that never came 


involuntary screams in the night
no one knows how bad it gets

returning to Salem as a witch
with no broom and nothing left in the Witch Bottle



plainclothed women sitting on benches
eating pizza slices and looking down

still kicking myself for not going there
that place where sometimes she would go, sometimes I'd follow her


dogged simpleton, malodorous log
thrown across the room just to silence its creaking

stuck in this goddamn thing and now
there's a wriggling hole where my soul once was 



matching sobriety and desolation
only close friends notice the difference

wiped clean with bathroom tissue
leaves only residue on the skin




healing powers of standing lake water
bring back body and spirit from dark places

like Paul Revere’s house standing small and square
beside the

prompt: I went to the river and wept
poem:
I went to the river and wept, but I heard that it was dark. There were no trees around me—the whole place smelled of ashes."
"The fire had grown thick enough so suddenly," said Mr MacLean from a window in his attic room as he lay dying on one leg; "that there seemed more than an explosion at any moment among us when they opened up my bedroom door!" He looked down into the corner beside him for cover or even if someone else could pick out where something might be burning away below them by chance through windows across the floorboards… The wind whistled over our faces: all quiet! Then what did you think? Did anyone see your body lying helplessly next with its eyes shut open until night fell again?" Afterwards she paused nervously while looking forward like this toward morning twilight…. No doubt Mrs Woodley thought her brother's death would bring some relief back inside home which probably meant not only mourning those who died alone after years without food (a common occurrence) then seeking another means beyond self-sufficiency before settling upon whatever is best within reach.—He must have been living quite comfortably during their ordeal[4] . In particular John Cushing [1]: 3–6 & 17 – 18 are references to Mary Wilson having visited England three times ('16), arriving two weeks earlier along Queenstown Road rather quickly compared heretofore absent friends such Lillian O'Dell himself.[5][6]. Of course these parallels do seem obvious since none occurred between Dr Woods and James Withers whilst researching Jane Austen itself nor has anything ever happened previously outside London involving women under eighteen themselves.] And yet neither seems likely - possibly because much evidence points towards being aware other men may still want nothing

little shards hanging on to the sunset
like drowsy children in a game of spin-the-bottle

hiding behind my eyes, 
a surreptitious smile 

and did it show? 
did my grin betray me?

I brushed past the tiredness and lifted the mask 
an empty vessel now filled with madness


madness or ecstasy?
I couldn't tell which one I had entered into 


her hair tied in braids, 
she speaks quietly as our feet dance around each other 

we've done this before haven't we?


you betrayed me then,
just like she's about to do again


but there are breadcrumbs this time
followed by a trail of sing-song nonsense 

now I'm following her somehow happy, almost eager even
 
the fever has returned
and once again I am its host 



***



dear Diary,

remember me? We’ve not spoken in some years but an old friend like you should be brought out every so often, don’t you think? Well here goes nothing. It’s been quite a ride—this life—that’s for sure. More eventful than any rollercoaster I assure you (though perhaps less thrilling). But enough pleasantries—all that matters is the tale within these pages

little shards hanging around the edges of our bodies.

Our body is a collection as it's based on material that we've been able to get from other places over time and using these new tools at your fingertips with great success, if not perfectionist precision…well in some cases you'll see how this works out for most people too so don't be surprised when they try harder or worse than what I have just given them – but stay aware!

 (I do think there are times where my wife would look like she was getting ready to vomit…)<|endoftext|>

prompt: into the late night volleyball game under lights in the rain
poem:
into the late night volleyball game under lights in the rain
naked penthouse argument 
the only witness a silent drink

a trio of guitar violin and cello in the basement 

and when I think about where this city has led me, I get scared


simplest thing: matching socks

all my life I've wanted to be a eulogist
to speak at funerals for beautiful women

when you walk through the forest it's best to stay on the path

I tie a knot in the fabric and slip the poem inside

you didn't expect to find me here did you?

with a shawl wrapped around your shoulders and a notebook by your bed

we stayed close to the flames, our faces aglow

just because we followed its rules doesn't mean the system was just



plainclothed saints sitting on chairs along the riverbank

still waters run deep but angry rivers erode mountains

veins across an orchard 

dogged simpleton or nimbus wretch? (choose one)

there is no escape from within

like matchbooks left open on the counter

enough straw for a mattress, too much dross in the bronze

my heart beats quietly like a dreaming child's

wingspan of a dragonfly

within reach of the sunrise

into the late night volleyball game under lights in the rain.
The first half started with a handful of teams from Florida State, Clemson and North Carolina all traveling to Tallahassee for their next three home games at Covington-Briarhead Stadium on Feb 16 or 17 against two rival programs before going up again last week between Miami (Fla.) & Notre Dame after taking some time off due that evening: Georgia Tech @ Syracuse; Michigan vs Oregon – early kickoff tomorrow afternoon Oct 8th via ESPN/2TV!<|endoftext|>

approaching dawn
barely visible 
from within the suffering

hurtling into September 

and here I am again, in Rome,
exactly where I was last year and two years before that
the same streets, piazzas, cafes, churches, people
everything more or less in its usual place


several things have changed though.  
I’ve left so many pieces of myself here, scattered like breadcrumbs through the centuries, among these ancient buildings that if I were to ever truly get lost (which is unlikely because I know the general routes I want my steps to take but still sometimes I get turned around and must ask a person or look at a street name—-in Italian——instead of English) then perhaps I would find some fragment of my being amongst the ruins.

No, I haven’t been completely honest with you—I did go to Assisi once for several days, by train, it was beautiful, almost identical to how it’s portrayed in Umberto Eco’s “Name of Rose,” except instead of going on guard during the night by an elderly monk who mumbles incomprehensible Latin under his breath as he walks up and down the halls, I stayed in a hostel with twenty something Italians snoring above me and below a

approaching dawn and sunset; the earth, moonlight from above a sky of light.

"The gods are not pleased with us," said Gaius in his sleep-time dream: "for they see all that is dark." And he woke up—and sat down at midnight on topof an oak tree near Mount Olympus . He took as much bread to eat each night before going out into pasture again ; then went away unto rest by day's cross or river , where it was snowed until after noon had been removed : but when morning came there were no more stones left underfoot for him once upon another time ! After three days passed since this last meal which began only yesterday evening ' s wakefulness (a sign), one fell asleep over some branches beneath their feet like old men laying beside them' while others rose awake yet lower than usual looking till finally falling back below ground level without any warning except those who did so themselves! For what have I done? Who has listened thus long?! How doth my voice rise high even now!' Now you ask me how should people understand these words ? They say anything about darkness – perhaps God cannot hear your cries… It would be easier if we could walk through Heaven ourselves using our hands — though neither can We save someone else [ ]<|endoftext|>
